title: Stats and what you should know about them.
body:
Stats should never bee seen as the be all end all and this is why when seeking them you have to consider a few things. 

-how old are the stats? If they were 5 yrs old add another 5-10 yrs for those stats to even be gathered. There are treatment advancements during that time that alters those stats or may not even be considered. 

-  how is stage 4 aka distant metastatic spread recorded in your country and for your particular cancer? For example in Canada and the USA the stats for stage 4 breast cancer only includes those who were metastatic de novo (stage 4 right at diagnosis). Where as anyone who was initially early stage and then developed mets are still grouped in with their initial staging group. This skews the stats significantly. So someone looking at early stage stats will see that the survival rate is anywhere from 72%-100% (stage 3 to stage 0). When infact early stage breast cancer does not kill ever. The only stage that kills is stage 4 aka metastatic breast cancer which is 100% terminal.  And yet even stage 4 shows a 22% survival rate and that brings me to my next issue. 

- you are considered a survivor no matter the stage after 5 yrs. Even if you die year 5 day 1 you are no longer counted in the stats and you are considered a survivor even for those of us who are outliers and have estimated life expectancy above 5 yrs but still require full time active treatment. 

Stat collection needs to be adjusted and even then there is always going to be a margin of error. 

Honestly take it with a grain of salt. According to stats I should have been dead 2 yrs ago and here I am still kicking and still on my 1st line 4+ yrs in.
